rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF TIMOTHY ANDREW TALBOT
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Demurrer Of Eumene Ching To Petition For Determination Of Entitlement To Estate Distribution Under Probate Code 11700 (Added For Posting Of The Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: [Part 2 of 2] Probate Code section 8270 sets the statutory period in which "any interested person, other than a party to a will contest and other than a person who had actual notice of a will contest in time to have joined in the contest" may petition the court to revoke the probate of a will. An action to contest a will may be brought to contest the legal validity of a will based on lack of proper execution, or lack of testamentary intent or capacity, or undue influence, fraud, duress, mistake, or revocation. (see, e.g. Prob. C. section 8252.) Facially, the Petition is not brought pursuant to section 8270, and does not facially plead or allege the Will admitted to probate is invalid or should not have been admitted to probate. The Petition does not plead or allege Talbot was a party to a prior will contest. As pleaded in the Petition, Talbot and Ching both presented the Will for probate in their original petitions. Instead, facially, the Petition is brought pursuant to Probate Code section 11700. Under section 11700, " At any time after letters are first issued to a general personal representative and before an order for final distribution is made, the personal representative, or any person claiming to be a beneficiary or otherwise entitled to distribution of a share of the estate, may file a petition for a court determination of the persons entitled to distribution of the decedent's estate. The petition shall include a statement of the basis for the petitioner's claim." Unlike a petition seeking to contest the validity of a will or revoke a will's admission to probate, a petition to determine distribution rights may be filed at any time after letters are first issued and before an order for final distribution is made, and by "any person claiming to be a beneficiary or otherwise entitled to distribution of a share of the estate." The Petition was filed within the time-period allowed under section 11700 -- Letters testamentary have issued and an order for final distribution has not. The Petition facially alleges Petitioner is decedent's brother and heir at law and pleads a legal theory under which decedent's Marital Dissolution Agreement effects decedent's estate planning instruments in such a way that Petitioner stands to inherit from the estate through the laws of intestacy. Whether the Petition's legal theory or claims are correct is not before the court in ruling on a demurrer. Assuming as true the facts properly pleaded and properly noticed in the Petition, facially brought pursuant to Probate Code section 11700, the claims do not necessarily fail on the basis of res judicata, the statute of limitations under Probate Code section 8270, or lack of standing. Petitioner shall lodge with the clerk in Department 204 by the time set for this hearing a proposed order consistent with this tentative ruling as published above. Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise other parties and the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) no later than 4:00 p.m. on the day before the hearing. To appear by Zoom, go to zoom.us and click "Join Meeting." Enter Meeting ID number 1602254765 and passcode 514879. To appear telephonically through Zoom, call 1-669-900-6833 and follow the prompts. Enter Meeting ID number 1602254765# and passcode 514879#. Parties appearing via Zoom should use their full names in the "Your Name" section. Counsel and parties may appear by audio-only telephone through CourtCall by calling CourtCall at 1-888-882-6878 and obtaining an appearance access code for the hearing's scheduled date and time in Department 204.

rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF TIMOTHY ANDREW TALBOT
rulings.Calendar Matter: Demurrer Of Eumene Ching To Petition For Determination Of Entitlement To Estate Distribution Under Probate Code 11700
rulings.Rulings: [Part 1 of 2] This tentative ruling is issued by Judge Ross C. Moody. Before the court is Demurrer of Eumene Ching to Petition for Determination of Entitlement to Estate Distribution Under Probate Code 11700 ("Petition"). A general demurrer to the Petition is brought on the grounds of res judicata; the 120-day statute of limitations under Probate Code section 8270; and Talbot's lack of standing to bring the Petition. Code of Civil Procedure section 430.41(a) requires either in-person or telephonic meet and confer conferences prior to filing a demurrer. There is no declaration in support of the demurrer showing the demurring party complied with the meet and confer requirements of Code of Civil Procedure section 430.41. Demurrer of Eumene Ching to Petition for Determination of Entitlement to Estate Distribution Under Probate Code 11700 is OVERRULED, for the follow reasons. A demurrer is a pleading used to challenge the legal sufficiency of an opponent's pleading based on defects that appear either on the face of the pleading under attack or from matters outside the pleading that are judicially noticeable. (See Blank v. Kirwan (1985) 39 Cal. 3d 311, 318; County of Fresno v. Shelton (1998) 66 Cal. App. 4th 996, 1008, 1009.) The issue on a demurrer is whether, assuming as true the facts properly pleaded and properly noticed, do the challenged claims necessarily fail. (See Code Civ. Proc., section 430.10(e); Blank v. Kirwan (1985) 39 Cal.3d 311,318.) "[I]f, on consideration of all facts stated, it appears that plaintiff is entitled to any relief against defendant, complaint will be held good, though facts may not be clearly stated, or may be intermingled with statement of other facts irrelevant to cause of action shown, or though plaintiff may demand relief to which he is not entitled under facts alleged." (Augustine v. Trucco (1954) 124 Cal.App.2d 229, 236, quoting Matteson v. Wagoner (1905) 147 Cal. 739, 742.) A demurrer must demonstrate no right to any relief under the cause of action. (See Augustine, supra, 124 Cal.App.2d at p. 236.) The running of a statutory time limit must appear "clearly and affirmatively" from the face of the complaint. (Committee for Green Foothills v. Santa Clara County Bd. Of Supervisors (2010) 48 Cal.4th 32, 42; Stueve Bros. Farms, LLC v. Berger Kahn (2013) 222 Cal. App.4th 303, 321.) Neither party has asked the court to judicially notice any matters and the court does not take judicial notice of any matters on its own motion. Here, the Demurrer's res judicata, statute of limitations, and standing arguments appear to be premised on reading the Petition as a post-probate will contest brought under Probate Code section 8270, and the admission of a will to probate as a "final judgment on the merits," specifically a final judgment as to the validity of the will. The Demurrer argues Petitioner's withdrawal of his competing petition for letters of administration and the court subsequently granting Ching's petition for letters of administration has created a "final judgment on the merits" regarding the validity of the Will. [Part 1 of 2]
